data.title: Vistra: Undeniable Positives Despite The Downward Drag
data.description:
With its increasing interests in nuclear energy, Vistra Corp. (VST), had a stellar 2024, followed by solid gains until H1 2025. However, it's hard to ignore that the stock is down by over 16% since I last checked on it in July. Considering that its short-term market valuations were stretched at the time, the drop isn't surprising.

But it does make a case for another look at the stock for two reasons. One, to assess if the price has dropped enough to make VST attractive for short-to-medium term investors now. And two, to scan for any adverse developments that could have triggered the stock correction. I will look at the stock from the nuclear energy lens here, in a continuation of the theme from my previous articles.

Comanche makes progress, but not enough

Leading the stock's gains, 2024 was a good year for Vistra from a nuclear energy perspective as its full acquisition of the subsidiary Vistra Vision more than doubled the company's nuclear energy production YoY to 26% of the total. However, concerns regarding the future of its nuclear energy assets cropped up in early September as Jefferies downgraded the stock from Buy to Hold citing the lack of any data centre related agreement for its Comanche nuclear power plant.

The company soon assuaged some of these concerns, saying it had now entered into a 20 year power supply agreement. A supply of 1,200 MW of power, starting in the final quarter of 2027, to a client that's yet undisclosed is envisaged.

However, it's not an entirely satisfying piece of development. First, the fact that the client is undisclosed implies that it's probably not a data centre deal with a big tech company. Previous such deals, like Talen Energy's (TLN) with Amazon (AMZN) and Constellation Energy's with Microsoft (MSFT) have made big news by comparison. Next, the current deal covers only 50% of Comanche's capacity. As of now, it remains to be seen whether this demand will rise over time.

Positive policy developments

However, the developments at the company alone is just one part of the Vistra story. There's still plenty of policy support for nuclear energy which can provide momentum for the stock moving forward. A good recent example is the US government's encouragement to the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) to speed up the process of grid connections for data centres.

With nuclear energy making big recent gains as a clean and reliable source of electricity provision for data centres, this can be a key development. Projections of increased electricity requirement for AI at data centres is expected to increase US energy demand and ramping up their connections may well be just the next push that nuclear energy needs, which in turn is a positive for Vistra.

Other policy developments regarding nuclear energy, even if tangential, can also build the overall momentum for stocks in the sector. One of it is the recognition of uranium as a critical mineral, which can ramp up its mining.

Also, recall that the US government aims to buy stakes in critical minerals companies to secure the country's energy supply. It's purchase of a stake in MP Materials (MP) earlier this year sent the stock skyrocketing and even buoyed the rare earths sector.

With uranium now becoming a critical mineral, the sector's prospects could further improve, adding greater value to stocks in the sector. It does need to be added though, that Vistra is a buyer of uranium, so if the physical commodity's price rises rapidly, it can hurt the company's margins.

Softer GAAP performance, stronger guidance

The company's performance this year has been lacklustre, though, with revenues flat for the first nine months of 2025 (9m 2025), while the net income declined by almost a fourth YoY. While part of this was driven by fundamentals, as there was an outage at the company's coal fired Martin Lake Power Plant but it was also due to mark-to-market losses on derivative positions.

However, on a non-GAAP basis, the company showed better performance, with the adjusted EBITDA up by 14.1% YoY for 9m 2025. In fact, the adjusted EBITDA guidance was narrowed to USD 5.7-5.9 billion, which brings the midpoint higher by 1.75% compared to the previous guidance. Further, the company also initiated the guidance for 2026, with expectations of a 24% increase in adjusted EBITDA at the midpoint.

Unconvincing near-term case...

The stock's market multiples remain unconvincing, however. The forward EV/EBITDAs for 2025 and 2026 are at 12.9x and 10.4x respectively, assuming that the adjusted EBITDA comes in at the midpoint of the guidance range. Both are higher than the stock's five-year average of 9.75x.

VST does better when the forward P/E ratio is considered instead. To estimate the net income figure, here I assume that the net income to adjusted EBITDA ratio remains unchanged at 43.53%, as seen for 9m 2025 remains, for the full year 2025 and also for 2026. This results in forward P/Es of 22.1x and 17.7x respectively. With the stock's five-year average at 19.67x, the P/E clearly subsides enough to make something of a Buy case for the stock next year.

On the whole, though, considering both the forward EV/EBITDA as well as the P/E, the short-term case for the stock just isn't convincing. Over the medium term, however, it can become more attractive. For two reasons:

  • The company sees the opportunity for the adjusted EBITDA to be between USD 7.4-7.8 billion in 2027. Assuming that this eventually doubles as the guidance as well, the forward EV/EBITDA declines to 9.8x indicating fair valuation on this metric.
  • Next, as the table below shows, the consensus of analysts' estimates put the forward P/E for 2028 at 12.76x, which actually reflects undervaluation and suggests a 55% upside to the stock from the current levels. I'd take this with a pinch of salt though, considering that earnings have consistently surprised on the downside, as discussed in greater detail in the Earnings risk section below.

Long-term case

The long-term case for Vistra continues to look good. McKinsey & Company estimates that power demand in the US will increase at low single digit percentage up to 2040 (see graphic below) due to "reindustrialization, AI, data centers, and electrification". This is a pick up in demand after two decades, which can hold Vistra in good stead.

This is quite likely baked into analysts' expectations from the stock, with forward P/E expected to reduce further to 10.89x by 2030. Additionally, the dividends are worth considering. Even though VST's forward dividend yield at 0.56% is as underwhelming as they come, the five year yield on cost is at a not too shabby 5%.

Earnings risk

The key risk to the upside for VST in the medium and long term is from the earnings projections. As the chart below shows, there's no outlier to the trend of a negative earnings surprise for the company. As a result, it can be expected that the forward P/Es would wind up higher in the future than is currently forecast, as earnings turn out lower.

Still, it's some comfort that the medium term Buy case for the can stock sustain. Assuming that the downwards earnings surprise is 23.6%, the average of the past three years, in 2028, the company would clock an EPS of USD 9.7, which translates into a forward P/E of 16.7x. This still indicates undervaluation, though the upside now reduced to less than 20%. It does follow that the stock remains undervalued over the longer term, however.

It's worth bearing in mind though, that over the past eight years, as the chart below shows, the earnings miss has also been bigger at almost 58% in 2018. So, investors need to brace for the possibility going forward.

Investor takeaway

To go back to the starting point, yes there are good reasons for the correction in the stock. For one, its nuclear energy production has probably not found takers in data centres to supply power to so far, which are fuelling energy demand. Also, the the company's short-to-medium term forward P/Es can be unconvincing right now. And this considering that earnings have significantly surprised on the downside over the years.

But the arguments stacked in its favour are undeniable too. The company's growing nuclear energy production at a time of demand for the sector, its recent deal for the Comanche power plant, the US's growing demand for energy, the policy push for energy generation and security, the company's positive adjusted EBITDA outlook, favourable long-term P/E and dividend yield all work in its favour.

All things considered, there's still a strong long-term Buy case for Vistra, with clear tilting of winds in the favour of nuclear energy. And to give the company it's due, its recognition of the same leading to an expansion in its nuclear fleet. I'm retaining a Buy rating for the long-term, though short-to-medium term investors could benefit from a bigger price correction.

data.title: Hercules Capital: Diamond In The Rough
data.description:
Over the last couple of months, various BDCs have experienced drawdowns due to worries about a private credit bubble. I tend to agree with that sentiment when it comes to many BDCs, but often the baby gets thrown out with the bathwater, and you find a diamond in the rough like Hercules Capital, Inc. (HTGC).

Unlike most BDCs, Hercules Capital operates in a niche it dominates: venture debt. This positioning provides superior deal flow, a structurally lower cost of capital, and embedded upside optionality through equity-linked features. Importantly, venture debt exposure also avoids many of the credit stresses currently emerging in mid-market LBO lending. As a result, the commonly cited risk of lower interest rates is overstated in the context of Hercules’s business model.

Portfolio

The first key thing that I'm looking out for in this environment is how exposed a particular BDC is to the private credit bubble and to private equity deals. This is because this is where most of the risk in private credit lies. Traditionally, what happened in private equity was that smaller companies would be bought for a low multiple, then rolled up and sold at a higher multiple. This financial engineering continued up the chain till you got to take the company public. So high PE returns were attributed to multiple expansion. Afterwards, there was only so much financial engineering that worked, so it shifted to operational improvements, but again, there are limitations there, too. Now the argument against PE is that it's just a leveraged version of public equities with large fees and a lack of liquidity, and that if/when things go south, BDCs that lent to these LBOs will suffer. I tend to buy into this argument. The great thing about Hercules is that it focuses on later-stage venture debt, where these same issues aren't present, and they make it clear that they're industry professionals in their niche, which provides them with an edge:

Source

Industry composition of their portfolio:

The industries are also ones that I'm not as worried about being hit by cyclicality (such as defence), or they have a convex payoff (such as drug discovery and development).

The convexity is particularly interesting since it's difficult for the average retail investor (myself included) to get exposure to venture capital, or if we buy public equities in certain industries like biotech, we have a harder time assessing them without domain expertise. The venture debt structure provides downside protection since debt gets a guaranteed payoff, unlike equity, alongside covenant protection. Plus, if the upside does play out, then warrants to participate. So it's the best of both worlds for investors who want exposure to venture debt without the domain expertise.

The warrant and equity portfolio isn't particularly large, but it has an asymmetric payoff. The debt portfolio is $4.07 billion, whereas the equity and warrants are valued at $212.9 million on a GAAP basis. So it's small relative to the debt portfolio, but the Q3 investor presentation highlights that 50% of warrants get monetised, and a percentage of those will do so with a long right tail, as is the norm in VC. That's where the real upside of participation happens.

The other thing I like about the portfolio is the low leverage, yet the returns aren't sacrificed.

Total assets: $4.4102 billion.

Total liabilities: $2.2203 billion.

Total equity: $2.1899 billion.

There's around a 1:1 debt to equity, which is a healthy figure for a well-run BDC. Yet even with this low leverage, the ROE hasn't been sacrificed:

Source

As seen in more recent years, ROE has been above 15%. The easiest way to think about ROE in this situation is that it's the compounding rate of NAV. So with a 15% ROE, NAV should grow around 15% per year (assuming dividend reinvestment), then you can translate that to a shareholder return based on the premium or discount to NAV that you initially buy at, and that you reinvest dividends at.

What makes for this low leverage, yet high ROE is the nature of VC debt. Unlike conventional BDCs that are doing LBO debt or mezzanine growth equity debt, VC is considered riskier, so the total return that credit gets underwritten at is higher. In an efficient market, the higher total return would get cancelled out by the higher default rate, but because of the domain expertise of Hercules Capital, it's not the case. In other words, there's more alpha generation capability due to the expertise and experience that this BDC has, which allows it to get better deal flow and weed out higher risk opportunities.

This gets me to credit quality:

Fitch Ratings: BBB- with a Stable outlook.

Moody’s Investors Service: Baa2 with a Stable outlook.

Kroll Bond Rating Agency: BBB+ with a Stable outlook.

DBRS Morningstar: BBB with a Stable outlook.

All of these are considered to be investment grade, with Fitch being the lowest tier of investment grade and Kroll being on the higher end.

This is also seen in their low non-accrual rate. In the last earnings call, the president said:

And we, again, did not have any grade 5 credits. In Q3, the number of companies with loans on nonaccrual increased by 1. We had debt investments in 2 portfolio companies on nonaccrual with an investment cost and fair value of approximately $52.2 million and $47.2 million, respectively, or 1.2% and 1.1% as a percentage of our total investment portfolio at cost and value, respectively.

Subsequent to quarter end, we successfully worked through and resolved the 1 new loan that was added to nonaccrual during the third quarter.

This statement is very precise once you parse the sequencing. The interpretation I have is that there was one non-accrual before Q3, briefly two during, then back to one at the time of the earnings call.

In the Q3 2025 10Q, the following was shown regarding non-accruals:

Source

It shows that 98.8% of the portfolio is performing. I presume this was when there were two non-accruals, so the percentage of the portfolio that's performing should be even better now.

This all goes back to the point of them maintaining low non-accruals, yet still a high ROE with reasonable leverage. For these reasons, I believe the portfolio is well set up as a long-term compounder.

Management

Another point that's favourable about Hercules Capital is the quality and structure of management. The company has been operating since 2003 and has preserved capital throughout the GFC, Covid, and the most recent VC downturn in 2022. The non-accruals being de minimis (under 1%) is the norm for this BDC. The other benefit of the management team is access to deal flow. Since they've built up a reputation in the space, they get access to the best deals (this is the moat) and, on the flip side, pay a low cost of capital to fund themselves:

The 6% and 6.25% notes were at the absolute peak in rates. I think if they were to issue similar notes right now, the rate would be around 5%.

Similarly, there's a persistent premium to NAV due to investor demand, and they can dilute at that NAV premium, which is a lower cost of capital than peers.

The other major positive of management is that it's internally managed. Because it's internally managed, incentives are aligned with total returns, unlike externally managed BDCs, where the incentive is to grow AUM to collect more in management fees.

Last quarter, the total employee compensation + expenses allocated to the adviser subsidiary = $16.168 million, which annualises to $64.672 million. Last quarter's total assets were $4,410.167 million. So the cost of management is 1.466% of AUM. This is about in line or even a bit under what an externally managed BDC would be.

Impact Of Rates

The conventional narrative is that lower rates are detrimental for BDCs, as most of their credit is floating rate at SOFR plus a spread. This is true to a certain extent, but I think it's overstated.

First off, much of this credit has interest rate floors on how low its rates can go. So, at least for the time being, that mitigates some of the risk. The idea behind interest rate floors is that they should protect you until your current notes need to be refinanced. Then your notes get refinanced into lower rates, so you can handle lower investment income on your credit.

The second thing to keep in mind is that as rates go lower, deal activity goes up. We're already seeing this with record fundings, YTD. This extra funding should offset lower rates.

The other key thing is that as rates go lower, credit spreads widen. Generally, lower rates are inverse to risk-taking appetite, so when rates go lower, and investors become more risk-averse, spreads widen. This wider credit spread allows for Hercules to charge a larger spread above SOFR. Right now, the high-yield OAS is close to lows not seen since before the GFC. So I expect that to widen as rates go lower.

Source

Risks

There are some key risks to be aware of. The two obvious ones that come to mind are a VC downturn and a broader economic downturn. Hercules Capital is ultimately leveraged to the health of the venture ecosystem, so if funding slows down, then portfolio companies might delay growth or defer financing. In this case, demand for VC debt declines and thus fewer originations. This also means fewer IPOs and exits, which leads to warrants going worthless and equity values down with no exit liquidity. A broader economic downturn would create exogenous risks since companies would face deterioration in their fundamentals, leading to deteriorating credit quality.

The other risk I see in the BDC ecosystem at large is that mainstream players like KKR, Apollo, Blackrock, Ares, Blackstone and so on, will compete in niche areas that Hercules lends into. These larger firms have lower funding costs and similar resources to compete, so they can wipe out the moat that Hercules Capital holds. I don't think this is likely, but it's a risk to watch for.

Another risk is the premium to NAV compressing. Price to book:

Source

The premium to NAV is a sign of confidence from the market, but during liquidity crunches like March 2020, it turns into a deep discount. I don't foresee this being a major risk.

The last major risk is interest rate path risk. Lower rates are not inherently negative, but the path matters. Rapid front-end cuts without wider credit spreads are the main risk. If rate cuts occur in response to macro stress, not normalisation, the credit environment could deteriorate faster than credit spreads widen.

Recap

To recap, their portfolio has lower risk exposure to the potential private credit bubble since they operate in VC debt, and it's in specific sectors where they have domain expertise. The nature of VC debt is such that there are higher total returns if risk is well-managed and there's convex upside participation via warrants and equity.

The portfolio also has low leverage yet still maintains a high ROE. And in the process, credit quality isn't sacrificed with investment-grade credit quality and low non-accruals.

The management is long-tenured and internally managed with aligned incentives and lower cost than an externally managed BDC.

Lastly, lower rates aren't as negative as many market participants make them seem. It's likely that lower rates mean more deal activity and wider credit spreads.

For these reasons, I've put a buy rating on Hercules Capital.

data.title: BAE Systems: The Overvaluation Is Clear
data.description:
BAE Systems plc (BAESY) has been on my watchlist and an investment for me for some years. In my last article, which at this time is more than a year ago, I managed to "time" the company's high before a price dip that persisted at the very least for a few months. You can find that article here, and we can see that occurrence here.

That being said, as you can see, the company has continued to outperform in the longer term, and I did not raise my targets for the company after the dip in 2025.

I did not purchase the company following the dip, unfortunately. I believe the entire sector to be significantly overvalued, and the company's dip over the past few months yet again confirms this overvaluation to me.

In this article, I'll be updating the upside, the expected performance, and the price we can estimate the company to have.

Defense industries in Europe (and also in the US, to some degree) have been surging for some time since the Russian invasion of Ukraine. This means that an industry that for some time had been in the lower multiple ranges due to the political nature of its spending and revenue trends. The trends we've been seeing have made analysts either forget this cyclical nature or assume that this represents a "new normal" for the sector and the companies in it. There's some support for this idea, but whenever a company or a sector moves up to the sort of multiples we're seeing here - 30-40x, compared to 10-15x or below that historically, this presents a marked increase in risk.

My question to investors still in this sector is, what they think will happen if and when the situation in Europe calms down, and the Russia/Ukraine situation is solved. Will the same sort of trends persists? There's no doubt that the initial, already-signed contracts will obviously pay dividends - but what about new contracts and orders?

This is what, I believe, should drive the forward valuations of these companies. Let's see what we have here.

BAE Systems: Pointing Out Inflated Valuation Both In The Market And The Company

It's been some time since I covered the company. The latest and biggest report was the half-year 2025A report, but we also have a trading statement for 9M25.

Let me put the company's position and the heart of my thesis as follows.

BAE Systems is a company that, during low growth years, traded at less than 10x P/E, and often went as low as 6-7x P/E, and that wasn't even during the GFC or any sort of financial downturn. Since that time because the company has seen double-digit AEPS growth for some time, we're trading at highs of close to 30x, and are now trading closer to 23x P/E. The 20-year average is still around 12x.

That means the company is trading more than 100% above its average P/E because over the past few years earnings have been up double-digits instead of single digits. The company's dividend hasn't meaningfully improved at the same rate (meaning it's not doubled).

So what's the underlying justification for the trend you see here?

I would argue there's very little reasonable justification for this. The company's results have not been good enough to justify this. Yes, solid growth - yes, good results. Yes, the company is positioned for a good opportunity with the global trends.

But no, the company's order trends are not showing the same sort of stability and growth that it's shown us previously. In fact, it's going down.

Orders declined by close to 10% for 1H25 YoY; sales were up, but FCF went into the negative YoY. Good qualities include the continued, high backlog (over 75B GBP), with decent visibility into the mid-2030s for its earnings.

And this might all be true - but does that mean that the company's earnings are going to keep growing from here on out? Forecasts might say yes - but I say that it's far more doubtful.

The US market is one of the most significant here. It's the largest defense market in the world. With upsides in the space sector, missiles and missiles, Counter-UAS, naval modernization, and Missile defense, the company has its hands full. Meanwhile, with Europe re-arming, and provided this continues despite a potential wind-down of the Russian war, then this would also mean solid income levels during the coming years.

Part of the problem, if you want to call it that (I do), is that the company is continuing to support its share price with the addition of buybacks. Buybacks at this pricing/valuation only serve to increase the inflation we're seeing here - and given the high price, it's (in my view), not an efficient use of capital for the company. But BAE Systems is not unique here - other defense companies are doing exactly the same thing, despite high valuations.

It's why I am mostly out of the entire sector.

Weakness is starting to appear in margins and earnings in some sectors as well. EBIT is down in Maritime as well as C&I, and operating business cash flow is seeing poor trends.

This quarterly/half-year trend also has to do with things like pension benefits, WC, and amortization of its intangible assets. Why is this so significant for BAE, especially?

The nature of its contracts and business, which is why I'm also somewhat more hesitant, dictates that payment terms and pension terms are very different than those in the private sector. In some periods, we're likely to see significant capital outflows without inflows, due to the timing of contract milestones (to name just one example).

As an example, the company saw a significant reduction in operating cash flow in the late 2000s due to significant advances from Saudi Typhoon. So, the reason that things might see a significant upside now could be because of significant early payments for contracts that the company has committed to undertake, which they then will have larger challenges to maintain over time, leading to earnings declines. These earnings declines would have little to do with less appeal and only to do with the way that the business and the contracts are structured.

This is just an example, but it shows us how a company in this sector can seem to be headed for a lot higher earnings, when in reality it's a peak that's likely to drop.

Also, it's worth mentioning that the UK and some EU countries have poor margins, and many of the company's contracts hail from these governments.

Let's look at the valuation and what sort of upside we can expect here.

Valuation For BAE Systems: Attractive Only If You Believe In Significant Premiumization

The primary risks here that I can see are two. First off, the company is prone to forecast uncertainty related to the company's dependence on the aforementioned contract trends. While income forecasts look positive, the positive forecasts must be put into the context of a forecast inaccuracy brought on by volatile contracts and how the payments and earnings for these are calculated. Consider that more than once in the past 20 years, BAE Systems has seen EPS declines of double digits or close to it, not at all related to broader market trends. Consider also that for nearly 10 years, the company went absolutely nowhere in terms of earnings (with an AEPS growth trend of nearly 0% between 2009 and 2018).

It's happened before, and that may happen again - and it was exactly during this trend that the company fell to about 6-7x normalized P/E.

Secondly, the company's current valuation marks a one-off trend, and not one I necessarily consider repeatable or one all that likely to hold. In late 2024, the company fell to less than 18.5x P/E, for some time, almost becoming investable again. That's why I was happy I went "HOLD" last year in October - it prevented a loss of nearly 15-20% in the short term, before the company bounced up again this year. Since October 2025, incidentally, the company is once again in a negative trend and currently trades at £17.2/share for the native ticker BA.

The forward AEPS growth is currently forecasted at about 13%. If this materializes, the company might be justified trading around the 18-20x P/E mark, but this is still a positive set of expectations, given that the 20-year average is about 60-70% below this. But even if it traded at close to 20x P/E, the upside would be less than 7% here.

In order, therefore, to have an upside of 15% annualized or above on a forward basis, this would require BAE Systems to manage 23x P/E until 2027E - and your annualized RoR wouldn't be above 16.5% even then.

How likely is this?

Based on recent contract terms and performance, not unlikely. Based on historical patterns, less likely. The fact is, even after dropping from almost 30x P/E to less than 23x, the company is still trading higher than it has for the past 20 years and more. It's fair, therefore, to say that BAE Systems has not really been this high for a very long time.

It's contrary to a risk-averse approach to invest in a company at this valuation. I'm forecasting BAE to a 20-year average P/E of 12.6x, or about £12/share for the native. That means a PT of £9/share is required to invest for a 15% annualized or above, which is almost a 50% drop from today's share price.

Risks are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

The high-level risks for BAE Systems are its dependency on large government contracts, which, as I mentioned above, have their own unique factors in how payments and income flows are done. Furthermore, there are risks related to supply chain dependencies, significant geopolitical shifts (which we've seen recently and we might see again).

To dig down a little deeper, the company's success depends on correctly estimating both long-term costs and long-term income trends. These are decade-long trends. So by saying that you believe the forecasts, you're essentially saying that BAE Systems is very successful, or doesn't have much risk in accurately estimating changes that change their margins.

I think such a forecast would be very hard to make - and thus, I consider this one of the company's primary risks, which I believe to be severe enough to discount the company further (see below for my targets for the ADR).

Thesis

The company is a solid international defense contractor with a presence in many attractive markets. It has a solid portfolio and a great long-term product upside. As a company, this business is as solid as it gets, with a well-covered dividend and great fundamentals.

The valuation is absolutely core when you're looking to invest in a company like this - and the current valuation wasn't "there" a year ago, and certainly isn't there now, when we're at almost 23-25x P/E. Because of this, I consider the company a "Hold" here - not a "Buy". I also consider the company's exposure to margin changes, which the company doesn't control (due to its contract structures). Based on this, I give the company a fair value target of £12/share and a PT of £9/share.

PT is $47 for the ADR, given that BAESY is a 4x ADR, to get a solid sort of upside here - and we don't have that, and this is updated for late 2025.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, but 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. Based on this, and a valuation target of $47/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me, I give the company a "Hold" rating as of December 2025.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Helen Of Troy: Opportunities In The Ruins?
data.description:
A little over a year ago, I believed that shares of Helen of Troy (HELE) looked cheap, as questions lingered around the Olive & June acquisition. Its business was hurt by inflationary pressures hurting consumers discretionary spending, while the business faced operational and execution challenges as well.

Even while facing issues, the company kept on buying stock while it announced a somewhat surprising nearly quarter of a billion acquisition of Olive & June late last year. This deal, and buybacks, were pushing up leverage quite a bit, as I questioned those capital allocation decisions.

Niche Consumer Brands

Helen of Troy is a leading global consumer products business that owns and operates a portfolio of well-recognized and trusted brands. These brands include well-known names like Oxo, Braun, Pur, Vicks, Honeywell, Drybar, Revlon, Curlsmith, and some others. Combined revenues came in at nearly $2 billion in recent times, split pretty evenly between beauty & wellness and home & outdoor products, a segment that the company created over the past decades.

The idea behind the business is that of owning brands that post strong growth and fat margins, holding relatively large market shares in niche segments in which local management teams are given a great deal of autonomy.

A $70 stock in 2015 rose to a peak of around $250 per share in 2021. In this period, sales rose from $1.4 billion to $2.2 billion, as operating margins rose by a few points and the business bought back about 15% of its shares. What happened was a reversal of the pandemic-related demand, supply chain distortions, as well as self-inflicted issues, causing shares to fall to $1.8 billion by now despite an inflationary period. This caused a giant sell-off in the stock, with shares down well over 90% to just $20 per share at this point, trading at the lowest levels since the Great Financial Crisis.

Big Issues

Hard to believe, but early in 2003, the company was already on its retreat, with sales reported down 7% to $2.07 billion, with adjusted earnings reported at $9.45 per share. The company reported another 3% decline in 2024 sales, while adjusted earnings fell to $8.91 per share.

The company guided for stabilization in these results for the fiscal year 2025, as prevailing earnings power and a $70 stock in the fall of last year looked compelling. However, there were some points worth noting, including the fact that if we back out stock-based compensation expenses, realistic earnings were reported at just $7 and change.

Moreover, the company indicated during the year that it was lagging compared to its promises, all while the company announced a huge $500 million buyback program, as well as a $240 million deal to acquire innovative nail care company Olive & June.

With net debt reported at $693 million already and adjusted EBITDA seen trailing $300 million in 2024, leverage ratios were reported at 2.4 times. That was ahead of buybacks and M&A, with that prospect making me cautious here, certainly as leverage ratios would exceed 3 times upon the deal.

The added debt and critical questions about capital allocation decisions made me very cautious here, as I had favored execution and some initial buybacks over M&A efforts.

The Plunge Continues

Over the past year, shares of Helen of Troy have collapsed another 70% to just $20 per share. A hoped-for and perhaps anticipated recovery has not come to fruition; in fact, the opposite has occurred.

In April of this year, Helen of Troy reported its fiscal 2025 results, which were weak. Despite deal-making efforts, reported sales were down about 5% to $1.90 billion. If we adjust for impairment charges and restructuring charges, operating profits came in at $208 million, for low double-digit operating margins, with adjusted earnings reported at $7.17 per share. If we back out $0.87 per share in stock-based compensation expenses, realistic earnings come in at just $6.30 per share.

Net debt was reported at $908 million, for a 3.0 times leverage ratio. With the situation being highly uncertain, including the headwind from tariffs, the company did not provide a full-year outlook for 2026, as it was clear that no solution was in sight.

Early in May, Helen of Troy announced that its CEO would resign immediately from the company. More bad news continued in July, when first-quarter sales were reported down nearly 11% to $372 million. Adjusted earnings plunged from $0.99 per share to $0.41 per share, and while net debt fell to $858 million, leverage ratios rose to 3.1 times as EBITDA imploded. In August, Helen of Troy announced that Scott Uzzell would step in as CEO of the company as of September, previously serving as general manager of Nike North America.

In October, Helen reported a near 9% fall in second-quarter sales to $432 million, with adjusted earnings cut in half from $1.21 per share to $0.59 per share. The company announced another large goodwill charge, similar to the first quarter. Net debt rose to just over $880 million, and with EBITDA trending downward, leverage ratios rose to 3.5 times. By the end of November, the company announced that it was amending its existing credit agreement, providing the company with an extended holiday regarding its maximum leverage ratio.

And Now?

With about 23 million shares outstanding, the market value of the firm has fallen to just $460 million, which, including net debt of $880 million, makes for a $1.34 billion enterprise valuation.

This shows the degree of the painful wreck on investors, with a total of about $1.5 billion spent on acquisitions over the past decade, while another billion was spent on buybacks. Both look pretty ill-advised right now in light of the prevailing valuation.

By now, the valuation has dropped well below 1 times sales and about 5-6 times EBITDA, which is not a demanding multiple. However, this comes with adjusted earnings and, of course, dwindling sales. Moreover, with so many brands in its portfolio, many of the issues facing the business are widespread, as the problem is not just in one of its brands or divisions.

For now, the company sees full-year sales down around 8% to $1.76 billion, with adjusted earnings per share down to just $4 per share, excluding nearly a dollar in stock-based compensation expenses. That, of course, looks very cheap, but it comes with highly adjusted earnings metrics and sky-high leverage.

Of course, a new CEO is in place who is looking to focus on streamlined operations, improve efficiency, and increase engagements, all while a few kitchen sink quarters have come in already. Moreover, the many brands in the portfolio make it so that divestments could be very accretive, helping to reduce leverage.

Right now the situation is highly uncertain in between debt and pressured operations, with tariffs causing real overhang on the businesses, as equity really represents a call option here. In between credit being amended, which most of the loans are only payable in 2029, the company still has time to resolve its issues, but they are severe.

However, a combination of improved cash flows in the second half of this year, divestments, and stabilization could easily stabilize the position here and create a real re-rating for the equity to reveal real upside here. For that, one has to be a believer with no green shoots seen, although that bravery at these levels could turn out to be really lucrative.

data.title: MercadoLibre's Ecosystem Strengthens, But Rerating Triggers Remain Absent
data.description:
When I had rated MercadoLibre (MELI) a Hold in October, the restraint was not centered around the quality of the business or opportunity size. The restraint was primarily around the balance of valuation and execution. I thought that valuations were already assuming a continued flywheel strength and was also particularly looking for margin expansion. Topline durability was not a concern.

Since then, valuations have become more reasonable for the quality of the business (reinforcing my restrained stance). Equally, the Q3 2025 results show signs of strengthening of the flywheel. Since both execution and valuations seem to have moved favorably toward what I was expecting, MercadoLibre deserves a re-assessment against my original constraints. My analysis shows that while the progress on both the valuation and execution front has strengthened MercadoLibre's thesis, the developments do not automatically qualify as a rerating trigger—although it is a much stronger Hold now.

Revenue Growth Better Distributed

Revenue growth continued to show up in Q3, with a 39% YoY increase. The growth story has been around for a while and was not a concern in my October thesis either. What has been evolving and further confirmed is the breadth of the growth.

GMV acceleration appears to be driven by a lower shipping fee, not likely due to subsidized pricing or promotions or price hikes. Brazil items sold accelerated from 26% YoY in Q2 to 42% YoY in Q3—that is volume flowing through the system. Management also noted clear acceleration in Chile and Colombia. Argentina remained resilient despite macro instability.

So GMV growth in Chile accelerated for the third consecutive quarter, while Colombia growth picked up more than 10 percentage points Q-over-Q, both trends driven by successful items.

This part of the thesis remains strong overall for MercadoLibre—expected strong results and not a new surprise catalyst.

Flywheel Evidence

Lowering the shipping fee clearly translated to volumes in Brazil, with record conversion, retention, and NPS. There was also an expected increase in the listing of lower-ticket items. On the cost side, logistics leverage finally became visible. Unit shipping costs in Brazil declined ~8% QoQ. Capacity expanded without unplanned fulfillment center additions. The Casas Bahia partnership further addresses a known weak spot in white goods and large appliances. Overall, the flywheel effects have started to show up stronger—particularly with logistics leverage no longer a theoretical discussion. I expect the flywheel to get only broader with e-commerce feeding into fintech and vice versa till the ecosystem benefits start showing up even stronger.

I also find the strong principality data encouraging. Principality (a measure of how central MercadoLibre and Mercado Pago are to users’ financial lives) increased by 11 percentage points in Brazil and 2 points in Mexico. This was attributed to higher usage of credit cards and remunerated accounts. The ecosystem lock-in matters a lot for MercadoLibre's thesis because it shows customers sticking to the ecosystem as a choice over just simple financial benefits. Investing in higher engagement across the ecosystem improves long-term optionality and enables the flywheel effect. So that should help digest short-term margin pressures. Overall, progress is visible in the flywheel mechanism but yet to impact margins visibly.

Maturing Fintech Story

Fintech was a swing factor in the original Hold thesis due to concerns around credit expansion and margin volatility. Q3 de-risks that leg to an extent. Older credit card cohorts (2+ years) are profitable now. Brazil is nearing a 50% profitable TPV. Credit cards were launched in Argentina with structural advantages (no fees and strong distribution). Net Interest Margin After Losses (NIMAL) compression was largely specific to Argentina, driven by temporary funding cost spikes tied to macro volatility. Trends were mostly stable elsewhere. Overall, credit risks appear to be better controlled, and the fintech economics are trending positive. Argentina remains a volatile exposure and will be watched as a case study in how MercadoLibre works through unfavorable macro environments.

Profit Margins - A Gating Factor

While MercadoLibre ticks the execution boxes elsewhere, profit margins remain an unresolved issue. The profit margins dropped further in Q3 to ~5.7%, while my earlier thesis was looking for improvements toward double digits. This improvement is not expected over the period of a quarter or two. Margin expansion will take time and will sequentially follow growth and principality strength.

MercadoLibre's management clarified this strategic game plan clearly in the earnings call:

We are not managing the business for short-term margin. We are managing for long-term value creation. And we think that if we continue to sustain the levels of growth that we are delivering, we are in the right track.

The Q3 drop in margins is potentially due to free shipping investments and logistics scaling costs. Credit card rollouts in new geographies could also mean margin pressures till they reach optimal traction levels. Such investments are value accretive in the long term and reduce customer acquisition costs, so margin pressures in the short term are understood and acceptable.

However, these are not one-off initiatives. Credit card expansion alone may happen over geographies, with each new region taking multiple quarters to reach maturity and contribute better toward profit margins. What my Hold thesis requires to graduate is either progress towards double digits or at least a stabilizing phase as proof of the growth working out from an economic perspective. Or, a lower valuation for underwriting the time and execution risks to reach that stage. Time, because every single new service launch may take several quarters to start validating the economics, and if done sequentially, will push out the validation for years. Similarly, there could be new offerings that do not age well, and execution will have to optimize the best setup for margins far later in the growth trajectory.

Valuations Entry Zone Further Away

Since it is getting clearer that sustained margin health is not going to show up anytime soon (although that could be a rerating catalyst and an entry point for me), valuations need to be more rewarding than the contraction from ~56x forward earnings to ~49x now.

This may appear to be a tempting entry point, having moved toward the lower range of valuations seen in the past couple of years, but what has changed over this period is a deferred margin validation story. The problem with MercadoLibre is that expectations are already for a ~50% earnings growth in 2026 and ~40% earnings growth in the next two years.

The embedded topline growth expectations do not worry me. But the earnings expectations will need margins to fire sooner than what the business model currently implies. The business strategy of growth and customer acquisition followed by margins makes a lot of sense, but the stock is expecting something else. This could lead to a few quarters of earnings misses (like in the past two), which could keep the upside potential in check. I feel a low 40s valuation will be seen soon, potentially over the next couple of quarters (due to earnings misses). That could be a good entry point if margins remain pressured. Or, improvements in profit margins could also get me interested.

data.title: QXO: Invest In The Man And Mission
data.description:
Investment Thesis

With the leadership of Brad Jacobs and an industry ripe for change, QXO is a top long-term growth pick as they look to get $50 billion in annual revenue within the next decade. Right now, QXO is in a prime position for growth, and the market is factoring in some, but not nearly enough.

The x-factor in this company is its leader, Brad Jacobs, who has one of the best track records on Wall Street. The most notable include United Rentals, a 200-bagger, and XPO, a 50-bagger. Famous for his playbook, he builds massive amounts of shareholder value by acquiring and consolidating fragmented, high-growth industries.

This time, he hit the nail on the head. The targeted industry is extremely fragmented, leverages macro tailwinds, and is lagging on technology. This gives Mr. Jacobs the opportunity to execute his playbook to perfection and, once again, create outsized returns.

They acquired Beacon Roofing in April of this year, targeting a company with demand tailwinds and less cyclicality. Progress has been made through modernization, providing productivity gains and margin improvement by leveraging AI.

Although the market is pricing in some growth, given QXO trades at higher multiples than peers, it isn't pricing in the full potential of a Jacobs-led venture. Currently, QXO is just beginning its journey, making an attractive entry point while its growth potential is undervalued.

Overview

QXO was originally founded in late 2023 with the goal of consolidating the fragmented building materials' distribution industry using scale, technology, and acquisitions.

Mr. Jacobs, the founder and CEO, is known for his rigorous, intense research on M&A targets and will never overpay. He often leverages the newest and best advancements in technology, forming a moat against competitors. People are also a key part of his strategy, as he only wants "A-players," ones who he couldn't imagine working without and is more than happy to overpay to keep strong talent on board. Using his repeatable, successful playbook, QXO has all it needs to be a major player.

By July 2024, they were able to form a war chest of $5 billion, which included an investment from Jared Kushner's Affinity Partners. Kushner, the President's son-in-law, was also placed on the QXO board of directors.

Although their initial target, Rexel, rejected their bid, they were able to acquire Beacon Roofing, the largest publicly traded roofing company in North America. This was a great first addition as Beacon offers limited tariff exposure, demand from severe weather events, and opportunities from repair and remodel activity. This gives Beacon less cyclicality through housing cycles since roofs aren't optional, making the demand sticky.

Since the acquisition, they have made one other offer for GMS, a major drywall, ceiling, and steel framing distributor, for $5 billion. However, they were outbid shortly after by Home Depot, leaving a warning sign for QXO as competitors look to slow their acquisition progress.

QXO has now had just over six months to work with their first acquisition, and in the Q2 report, Mr. Jacobs mentions, "The integration of Beacon is progressing well, and we've identified opportunities that exceed our initial expectations." After getting under the hood of Beacon, QXO realized there were more opportunities to improve than they realized. This included many of their top SKUs being out of stock, a lack of warehouse automation, and $200 million in pricing leakage. They have begun addressing these problems directly, leveraging AI and data to modernize systems and improve pricing.

Industry

To put it bluntly, the $800 billion building products' distribution industry was chosen for a reason.

First, the industry is highly fragmented. In North America alone, there are ≈7,000 distributors, leaving it ripe for consolidation. As the company scales and pursues more acquisitions, expect synergies to improve margins and growth to compound.

The industry is also lagging behind in technology, and when acquiring Beacon, they found that fast-moving SKUs (making up 80% of sales) were often out of stock, outdated systems, manual processes, and limited inventory visibility. An estimated $200 million was lost to inefficiencies at Beacon; however, this is now being addressed as Mr. Jacobs claims double-digit productivity gains, largely through AI, and improved pricing discipline. This lines up with what Mr. Jacobs did at his previous venture, XPO, where he implemented changes such as autonomous tech, real-time visibility, dynamic pricing, and route optimization.

The tailwinds also make the industry attractive, with multiple demand drivers expected to help sales. One of these includes the current housing shortage, estimated to be ≈4 million homes in the U.S. With the current administration's push for affordable housing, QXO should be a large benefactor. The current housing pool also has a median age around 40 years old, providing additional demand opportunity through remodeling and repair. Finally, with an expected increase in climate-related disasters, building materials will also be needed for repair. This isn't just another Jacobs venture; it's change coming to an industry with secular tailwinds and a need for advancement.

Strategic Hires

A large part of Mr. Jacobs's philosophy is having the right team around him and utilizing the latest technology to improve productivity. Recently, these have gone hand-in-hand, with the hiring of a Chief AI Officer, Ashwin Rao. These types of hires are on the rise, mostly in tech and healthcare, but lag in the building materials distribution industry. Prior to his current position, Rao served as the first head of AI at Target Corp. "where he led a team that developed mathematical models for pricing, merchandising, customer experience, supply chain logistics, and other critical functions." This directly translates into QXO's technological mission of wholly upgrading the current technology and using data to their advantage.

More recently, they hired Michael Dewitt as the chief procurement officer, who came after working eight years as the VP of International Spend Management at Walmart International. There, he drove billions in savings and was one of the first to use AI for sourcing. His initiatives of next-generation procurement technologies "more than doubled annual savings in three years." This will correlate directly with an industry that has lagged on many of these technologies for years.

Recent Results

So far, they have progressed through two quarters with Beacon, beating EPS and revenue in both quarters.

Mr. Jacobs likes to move fast, and this case is no exception. They made huge strides in Q2, such as rebranding every customer touchpoint, flattening organizational structure from nine to four layers, stricter pricing tools to protect against leakage, linking compensation to profitability, and implementing immediate replenishments of critical SKUs. Remember, those are just a few of the changes they've made so far.

In Q2, the press release mentioned they are "confident we will at least double legacy Beacon EBITDA organically." They maintained that outlook in Q3, stating they "are firmly on track to organically grow legacy Beacon's EBITDA to more than $2 billion." Beacon had more issues than they initially thought; however, this isn't a setback. It's a realization of increased opportunity leading to a higher goal. To ultimately reach $2 billion in EBITDA, QXO is leveraging technology such as warehouse automation, something used at XPO, as well as utilizing AI. Double-digit productivity gains have already been provided by utilizing AI-focused tech in inventory management, pricing, and logistics. All of this provides support for not only their Beacon EBITDA target, but also their overall goal of $50 billion in annual revenue.

This has gone under the radar, with the stock remaining flat since June, ignoring results from both quarters under QXO management. With the stated target and more-than-expected opportunity, the current valuation is only more attractive.

Analysts tend to be very bullish on the stock with an average price target of $32.20 (+51%) and no ratings below buy (19% buy, 81% strong buy). This comes with higher expectations, as analysts expect QXO to grow EBITDA at a 34% CAGR over the next five years versus the S&P 500 Industrials Index, which has grown at a 14% CAGR over the previous five years. Both Wolfe and Oppenheimer expect $4.5 billion in EBITDA by 2030, led by best-in-class technology for the industry and familiarity with the Jacobs' playbook.

Valuation

Originally, Beacon was bought for $11 billion, and QXO is currently worth ≈$15 billion, adding a premium for the hope of improved growth and future acquisitions. While QXO is still integrating Beacon, hindering margins, it trades at a forward P/E of 45.25x and a forward P/S of 1.4x. These metrics don't tell the full story, making the best measure of valuation EV/EBITDA.

For 2026, revenue is expected to be $10.86 billion, and in the most recent quarter, adjusted EBITDA margin came in at 11%. Assuming margin improvement, driven by technology investments and productivity gains, we can spread that 11% for FY26, getting EBITDA of $1.3 billion. Currently, S&P 500 Industrials trade at an average of 17x EBITDA, while growth from 2020 to 2025 was at a 14% CAGR. QXO is projected to grow EBITDA at a 34% CAGR through 2030, leaving room to demand a higher multiple of 20x. With outstanding shares of 674.4 million and net debt of $1.64 billion, the equity value comes to $24.4 billion, or a price per share of ≈$36.20. As a base case, that's my 12-month price target.

Risks

QXO has had some helpful surprises so far, including the improved opportunity at Beacon. However, they have also had some setbacks. This has been driven by Lowe's and Home Depot, which have attempted to slow acquisition progress. Home Depot outbid them for GMS, their third attempt at an acquisition. Just a few months after, Lowe's acquired their own building materials' distributor, Foundation Building Materials, for $8.8 billion in another attempt to slow QXO. Although this is something to consider, the industry is still highly fragmented and allows room for QXO to continue acquisitions. Home Depot and Lowe's could keep up the pursuit; however, it would come at the expense of their margins, something they might not be able to give up.

To achieve their goal of doubling organic EBITDA for Beacon, they will have to follow through on execution, something Jacobs and his team are accustomed to. During their logistics venture, XPO, they doubled two of their acquisitions' profits in three years. Although their goal for QXO is entirely possible, unexpected pressures from housing or weather could hinder their progress.

If unexpected macro pressures occur, acquisition progress could slow down their overall goal of $50 billion in sales by 2035. However, the experience of this team should not be underestimated, with 40 years of experience working through all types of economic conditions, they're unlikely to be stopped.

Conclusion

Brad Jacobs has used his playbook to create outsized returns and billions of dollars for over 40 years, with QXO being his latest venture. He has shown focus and commitment to QXO's goals by stepping down from chairman roles at GXO and XPO. Not only is the industry highly fragmented, but strong tailwinds exist due to a lack of housing, aged housing stock, and expected climate change. I highly recommend listening to a discussion with Mr. Jacobs about QXO and the industry on this Bloomberg podcast.

Strong progress has already been made with the acquisition and integration of Beacon Roofing with changes such as modernizing systems, warehouse management systems, and leakage protection. Additional opportunities have been identified, and Beacon EBITDA is expected to double organically over the coming years, solidifying their goal of $50 billion in sales annually in the next decade.

Unexpected competition has come from Home Depot and Lowe's, which have bought companies in QXO's acquisition sphere these past few months. It is something to consider; however, this can't be kept up forever with the scale of fragmentation in the building materials' industry. With a current war chest of $2.31 billion in cash and leverage potential, they are ready for acquisitions.

As a base case, factoring in Beacon improvement and some M&A activity, my price target for the next twelve months is ≈$36.20 per share, or an equity value of $24.4 billion. This isn't something new to Jacobs and company, as they've pulled off similar feats in the forms of XPO, United Rentals, and United Waste Systems. That's why I'm not just investing in the mission; I'm investing in the man.

data.title: Will Santa Claus Rally For Top AI Stocks?
data.description:
Registration is now open for our Top Stocks for 2026 event with Steven Cress, featuring his newest list of high-conviction picks designed to target long-term performance. Steve’s previous Top Stocks lists have demonstrated an impressive track record, so don’t miss out on this much-anticipated event.

Santa Rally 2025: AI Edition

The “Santa Claus rally” is the seasonal tendency for stocks to rise over the final five trading days of the year and the first two of the new year. Since 1950, this seven-day window has delivered positive returns roughly 77% of the time, with an average S&P 500 gain of about 1.3%, making it one of the market’s most consistently strong periods. Moreover, since 1950, the Santa Claus rally has never failed to occur three years in a row. After back‑to‑back losing years for the index during this period, the natural question is whether Santa’s magic will return this time around.

Source Link: Seeking Alpha

Since I wrote 3 Best AI Stocks For The 2025 Santa Rally, markets have been anything but quiet. The Fed delivered its third rate cut of 2025 and signaled that there would likely be only one additional rate cut in 2026. After a sharp pullback on Dec. 12, it appeared a rotation into defensive sectors might come to pass. However, equities quickly found their footing on recent CPI data that reinforced the disinflation narrative. Against this backdrop, the question for 2025 isn’t just whether the rally shows up again, but which segments of the market will continue to flourish in 2026?

One High‑Potential Cannabis Pick for Your Stocking

The Santa Claus Rally typically favors the year’s existing leadership, and AI and data center names are well positioned to benefit from the seasonal pattern. However, this year also brings a new twist as cannabis stocks have a major policy catalyst in President Donald Trump’s executive order pushing to reschedule cannabis from Schedule I to Schedule III. Before diving into potential AI names, SA Quant has provided a bonus cannabis stock given the recent news.

Village Farms International, Inc. (VFF)

  • Sector: Consumer Staples

  • Industry: Agricultural Products & Services

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 12/23/2025): 1 out of 177

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 12/23/2025): 1 out of 11

  • Market Capitalization: $417.02M

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

VFF has recently pulled back in a classic sell-the-news pattern following Trump’s rescheduling announcement, but that volatility does not diminish the stock’s underlying fundamental case. VFF runs large-scale greenhouses that grow and distribute fresh produce in North America and also operates cannabis businesses in Canada, the U.S., and select international markets. The company delivered a record Q3 2025, driven by strong Canadian cannabis performance and surging international medical exports. Management outlined an expansion of Canadian production capacity and positioned Europe as a key growth driver over the next few years. This is consistent with what we’re seeing from a Quant perspective, where FWD EBITDA growth stands at an impressive near 95% compared to the sector’s 4.45%.

VFF Growth Grade

These promising developments have helped support positive analyst revisions, reinforcing the bullish narrative around the stock. That positive sentiment has contributed to powerful price momentum, with shares delivering a remarkable 367% return over the past year.

VFF Momentum Grade

While the stock’s headline valuation screens at a modest premium to the sector, it still trades at a meaningful double‑digit discount on a forward GAAP P/E basis, offering a more attractive entry point than first glance might suggest. VFF’s blend of growth potential and financial resilience highlights its ability to turn one kind of greenery into another, with enticing implications for cannabis investors.

How I Chose My Top AI Stocks

For readers who have been with us for some time, it’s no surprise that many AI-related opportunities span multiple sectors and industries, so it can be limiting to focus on a single sector. With that in mind, I screened IT stocks using the Seeking Alpha screener by selecting “Sector & Industry,” layering on all five Factor Grades (Valuation, Growth, Profitability, Momentum, and EPS Revisions), then sorting for Strong Buys with B+ or better Momentum. I also revisited earlier picks from this year’s AI lists and checked them twice for continued Quant conviction.

1. Credo Technology Group Holding Ltd (CRDO)

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Semiconductors

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 12/23/2025): 8 out of 537

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 12/23/2025): 2 out of 68

  • Market Capitalization: $27.08B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

Credo has earned the distinction as my top-performing 2025 Alpha Pick and also ranks as one of my best-performing Top Stocks for 2025. The company has remained out of reach for many investors, having maintained a Quant Hold rating for the majority of July through November owing to its rich valuation. However, recent market volatility and its latest earnings have once again brought the stock back within reach. CRDO had a monster Q2 FY26, delivering $268 million in revenue, up 20% sequentially and 272% year-over-year with a 68% non-GAAP gross margin. The company’s growth story continues to impress, according to CRDO President, CEO and Chairman William Brennan:

“In summary, we now have five distinct high-growth connectivity pillars: AECs, IC solutions, including retimers and optical DSPs, zero-flap optics, ALCs and OmniConnect Gearbox solutions. Together, they'll give Credo a combined total market opportunity that we believe will exceed $10 billion in the coming years, more than triple where we stood just 18 months ago.”

​This is consistent from a Quant perspective, with the company showcasing an overall ‘A+’ Growth Grade supported by an EPS FWD Long Term Growth (3-5Y CAGR) that's nearly 335% above the sector median. CRDO has also demonstrated steady improvement in its profitability characteristics, climbing from a ‘D’ grade just six months ago to its current 'B.' The company possesses a net income margin that's nearly 449% above the sector median, underscoring Credo’s operating leverage and pricing power.

CRDO Profitability Grade

As expected, analysts are enthusiastic about the company’s earnings prospects, providing unanimously positive revisions. This vote of confidence, paired with CRDO’s stellar recent momentum, could spell potential for further upside as the company executes through 2026.

2. Micron Technology, Inc. (MU)

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Semiconductors

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 12/23/2025): 2 out of 537

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 12/23/2025): 1 out of 27

  • Market Capitalization: $311.03B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

I have written extensively about Micron, and its Quant story remains solid following its most recent earnings result, which incidentally provided a broad lift for the semiconductor industry. MU is another standout Alpha Pick and PQP stock that has returned 45% and 111% to the portfolios, respectively. Let’s start with the low-hanging fruit: Very few semiconductor stocks provide such an attractive entry point for investors. MU, a dominant player in the AI memory space, trades at an incredible 89% discount in terms of its FWD PEG. This discount is particularly remarkable in light of the stock’s superb price momentum, with shares returning 207% over the past year. Together, the delta between that valuation gap and performance strength suggests the market is underestimating its long-term earnings power.

MU Valuation Grade

The stock’s growth and profitability are also both ironclad. Highlights include a FWD EBITDA growth that's 581% above the tech sector, alongside a 23% ROE vs. the sector’s 6.30%. The company also sports a remarkable 53% EBITDA margin, significantly higher than tech stocks and its own five-year historical average.

MU Profitability Grade

MU’s recent wave of upward estimate revisions further complements its discounted valuation, growth, profitability, and momentum, making it a key leader in AI infrastructure as we head into 2026.

3. Willdan Group, Inc. (WLDN)

  • Sector: Industrials

  • Industry: Research and Consulting Services

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 12/23/2025): 4 out of 617

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 12/23/2025): 1 out of 39

  • Market Capitalization: $1.67B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

I like Willdan as an unconventional AI play. The company neither sells chips nor networking hardware, but instead tackles the energy efficiency challenge from a professional services and engineering angle. This positions the company to benefit from AI-driven power demand without competing directly in crowded semiconductor or cloud markets. It also makes the company more resilient to tech volatility: Willdan has weathered the late Q4 turbulence well, returning well above the market and broader tech sector (XLK) on a trailing one-month basis.

WLDN vs. S&P500 vs. XLK trailing one-month return

The company’s revenue lines are well diversified, with strategic acquisitions expanding its capabilities and geographic reach. This approach is reflected in the company’s ‘A’ Growth grade, which includes an FWD EPS growth that's 644% above the sector median.

WLDN Growth Grade

One benefit of Willdan’s public‑sector client base is that longer-duration contracts create a recurring revenue base. This helps protect the company from news-driven tech volatility and has shored up its profitability: WLDN sports an ROE that is 31% above the sector median. With solid fundamentals and stellar timeliness indicators, WLDN is a potentially overlooked AI play that could also serve as a diversifier in the New Year.

Concluding Summary

The Santa Claus rally has historically delivered positive returns. The rally, which failed to materialize in 2023 and 2024, has never missed for three consecutive years, typically favoring the dominant investment themes, which today are AI and cannabis stocks. SA Quant has identified four Strong Buy stocks for your Christmas wish list this year. Village Farms International, Credo Technology Group, Micron, and Willdan Group offer an outstanding blend of investment fundamentals, momentum, and earnings revisions grades across the cannabis and AI spaces.

We have many stocks with strong buy recommendations, and you can filter them using stock screens to suit your specific investment objectives. Consider using Seeking Alpha's 'Ratings Screener' tool to help find stocks that achieve diversification into the desired sectors you like. Alternatively, if you're looking for a select number of Quant Strong Buy recommendations on a monthly basis, you might want to explore Alpha Picks.

If you're looking for more frequent investment ideas, we launched the PRO Quant Portfolio in June. It's a weekly rebalanced selection of our Top 30 Quant Strong Buy stocks, spanning multiple regions and market caps
